answer: Python's division operator (/) does not return an integer; it always returns a float. The two alternative operators are // for floor division (which returns an integer result) and % to calculate the remainder.

Python automatically concatenates two or more string literals placed next to each other, but this does not work with variables or expressions. If you attempt to concatenate a variable and a string literal without using the + operator, a SyntaxError occurs. To concatenate variables or a variable and a literal, you must use the + operator.
Out-of-range indices used for indexing in Python strings result in an error (IndexError: string index out of range), while out-of-range slice indexes are handled gracefully and do not raise an error.
Four numeric types are explicitly mentioned: int, float, Decimal, Fraction.

If you assign a list to another variable and then modify the original list, any changes you make to the list through one variable will be seen through all other variables that refer to it. To create a separate copy of the list and avoid this behavior, you can use slicing to make a shallow copy: new_list = original_list[:]
